summary: Recipient of the recommendation letters Fam. 13.43 and 13.44, a governor (apparently a provincial magistrate) to whom Cicero commends L. Oppius and the affairs of L. Egnatius Rufus, c. 58 BC. Praenomen and nomen are uncertain: the manuscript opener reads "Quintius Gallus" / "GALLO", commonly normalized to Q. Gallius.

Lucius Oppius, son of Marcus, a businessman (negotiator) operating at Philomelium in Phrygia and a close friend of Cicero. He managed the affairs of the banker L. Egnatius Rufus; commended to Q. Gallius in Fam. 13.43 and 13.44. Distinct from C. Oppius, Caesar's agent.

A proconsul named Curius, recipient of the one-line recommendation Fam. 13.49 commending Q. Pompeius Sex. f. Identity uncertain; the Perseus TEI for this letter is mislabeled n=159. Not to be confused with the banker M'. Curius of Patrae (person:mn-curius-patrensis).

Quintus Pompeius, son of Sextus, an old and close friend of Cicero who had repeatedly relied on Cicero's recommendations to support his fortune, standing, and authority. Commended to the proconsul Curius in Fam. 13.49. Distinct from Q. Pompeius the consul of 141 BC (person:q-pompeius-cos141).
A town in Phrygia (Asia Minor), on the road through the province; the place where the businessman L. Oppius conducted his trade (Fam. 13.43).
A city on the southwest coast of Cyprus, famed for its cult of Aphrodite. Cicero commends its people especially to the quaestor C. Sextilius Rufus in Fam. 13.48.
